3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This article is part of my SOLID Principles in Angular series, where I break down each SOLID principle from an Angular engineer’s perspective using practical examples and real-world Angular scenarios.
In this article, we’ll focus on the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP).
Like the other SOLID principles, this one is simple to understand in theory, but applying it consistently in real-world applications requires experience and thoughtful design.
When used correctly, LSP helps us build Angular applications that are easier to maintain, scale, and evolve as business requirements grow. It’s time to break it down and see how it applies to modern Angular development.
What does the Liskov Substitution Principle say?
Child classes should be replaceable with parent classes without breaking expected behavior.
In simple terms, if class B extends class A, then we should be able to use B anywhere we use A — without unexpected behavior or errors.
This principle sounds straightforward, but it becomes very important when designing class hierarchies.
Violating LSP usually means one thing:
Your inheritance model is flawed.
Classic Example
One of the most famous examples is Bird and Penguin.
class Bird {
fly() {}
}class Penguin extends Bird {
fly() {
throw new Error('Cannot fly');
}
}
At first glance, this seems reasonable.
A penguin is a bird, right?
But there’s a problem.
The parent class assumes all birds can fly.
Penguins cannot.
That means Penguin cannot fully behave like Bird.
This violates LSP.
Why is this bad?
Imagine another function depends on Bird.
function makeBirdFly(bird: Bird) {
bird.fly();
}This works fine for most birds.
But now:
makeBirdFly(new Penguin());The application breaks.
That’s the core problem LSP tries to prevent.
Better Design
The problem here is not with penguins.
The problem is with the abstraction.
We modeled Bird incorrectly.
A better design would separate flying behavior.
class Bird {}class FlyingBird extends Bird {
fly() {}
}
class Penguin extends Bird {}
Now the hierarchy makes more sense.
Only birds that can fly inherit flying behavior.
This design respects LSP.
LSP in Angular
In Angular, LSP issues usually appear when inheritance is overused.
This often happens when developers try to reuse logic through deep component inheritance.
Example:
BaseComponent
BasePageComponent
BaseDashboardComponent
UserDashboardComponentAt first, this may look clean.
It feels like code reuse.
But over time, problems start appearing.
Child components begin inheriting behaviors they don’t actually need — or worse, behaviors they cannot properly support.
For example:
class BaseComponent {
save() {}
delete() {}
}Now imagine a child component:
class ReadOnlyProfileComponent extends BaseComponent {
delete() {
throw new Error('Delete not supported');
}
}Problem?
The parent class assumes all children support delete.
But this child does not.
That’s an LSP violation.
The child cannot fully behave like the parent expects.
Real Angular Example
Imagine a base form component.
abstract class BaseFormComponent {
submit() {}
validate() {}
saveDraft() {}
}Now multiple components extend it:
UserFormComponent
ProductFormComponent
LoginFormComponentLooks convenient.
But then you realize:

• Login form doesn’t need saveDraft()

• Some forms validate differently

• Some forms don’t support draft saving at all
Now child components start overriding methods with empty implementations.
saveDraft() {
// not needed
}Or worse:
saveDraft() {
throw new Error('Not supported');
}That’s a warning sign.
It usually means the abstraction is too broad.
Why This Matters in Angular
Violating LSP in Angular often leads to:

• fragile architecture

• tight coupling

• hard-to-maintain component hierarchies

• unexpected bugs
This is especially dangerous in large enterprise applications.
Small changes in base classes can break multiple child components.
Debugging becomes painful.
Composition Often Works Better
This is one reason modern Angular architecture often prefers composition over deep inheritance.
Instead of forcing shared behavior through base classes, Angular gives us better alternatives:

• services

• directives

• standalone utility functions

• composition through reusable components
For example, instead of:
BaseFormComponentYou might extract reusable logic into:
FormService
ValidationService
DraftServiceNow components only use what they actually need.
That creates more flexible and maintainable architecture.
That’s it for this article. I hope this gave you a practical understanding of how the Liskov Substitution Principle applies to real-world Angular development. In the next article, we’ll continue the series by exploring the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) in Angular and see how it influences Angular architecture.
Thanks for reading, and I’ll see you in the next one.
